title: Schefflera angustissima
taxonomy: Schefflera angustissima is classified in the following manner: it belongs to the family Araliaceae, which is a family of flowering plants in the order Apiales. The genus Schefflera is a group of evergreen shrubs and trees native to tropical and subtropical regions. The classification of Schefflera angustissima can be broken down further into its kingdom (Plantae), division (Angiosperms), and class (Magnoliopsida). Schefflera angustissima is also known to be a part of the order Apiales, along with its closest relatives. This detailed classification provides insight into the plant's evolutionary relationships and place within the plant kingdom.
uses: Schefflera angustissima, also known as the dwarf umbrella tree, has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties in various cultures. The plant's leaves and roots are said to have antiseptic, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties. In folk medicine, it is used to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and digestive issues. Some studies suggest that the plant's extracts may have antioxidant and hepatoprotective effects, although more research is needed to confirm these findings. Further studies are necessary to fully understand the plant's potential medicinal applications.

side_effects:
Schefflera angustissima, like other members of the Schefflera genus, can cause gastrointestinal issues in some individuals if ingested, as the plant contains toxic compounds called saponins and calcium oxalate crystals.

The saponins can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, while the calcium oxalate crystals can cause oral and gastrointestinal irritation.

In severe cases, ingesting large amounts of Schefflera angustissima can lead to more severe symptoms, including abdominal pain, seizures, and even respiratory failure.

The skin and mucous membranes may also be irritated by the plant's sap, which can cause redness, itching, and burning.

It is essential to handle Schefflera angustissima with care and avoid ingesting any part of the plant to minimize potential side effects.
